title: Technical: The Canvas
subtitle: An interactive solution landscape of the key white paper concepts.
abstract: This paper assembles the various approaches in the white paper into a series of interactive canvases. Each scene is a data-driven, downloadable, bilingual diagram with animated agents and a spoken description, and each can be embedded on its own in the paper it comes from. Move through it, zoom in, and take the architecture away for your own development.

hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial cover image for the technical appendix titled "The Canvas". Muted Alberta cream palette with navy and rust accents. Archival print quality, no text, no logos.

slides.title: The Canvas
slides.caption: Technical appendix · paper 18
slides.subcaption: One interactive solution landscape for the whole approach.
slides.text: The Compression Problem argues that a written briefing cannot carry the fidelity of a system at scale, but a visual, spatial, interactive representation can. This appendix is that argument made literal: the whole approach assembled into one interactive canvas you move through, zoom into, and take away.

slides.title: Eleven views of one system
slides.caption: What the canvas holds

items.label: Estate, approaches, stack
items.desc: The legacy estate scanned, the four approaches, and the agentic technology stack.

items.label: Harness and factory
items.desc: The harness with its review agents, and the Pronghorn to Nexus to Velocity factory.

items.label: Game, hierarchy, future
items.desc: The delivery game, the inverted hierarchy, and the future-state modules.

items.label: Animated agents
items.desc: Agents move through each scene, so the structure is shown rather than described.

slides.text: The canvas holds eleven scenes, one per major idea in the collection, with agents animated through each. You step between them with the scene chips, drag nodes, zoom and pan, expand to full screen, and read or hear a description of what each scene shows.

slides.title: Built to be taken away
slides.caption: Data-driven, bilingual, accessible
slides.subcaption: Every scene is JSON you can download.
slides.text: Every scene is driven by a JSON record, so it can be downloaded, edited, and carried away as a standalone architecture. Each scene is bilingual, mobile-responsive, and carries a written narrative that serves both as the accessible description and as the script for a spoken explainer. The same scene component can be embedded on its own in the paper it comes from.

blocks.text: The [Compression Problem](/paper/eujjc) makes a claim: a written briefing cannot carry the fidelity of a four-hundred-and-sixty-six-million-line system to a decision-maker, because language moves at roughly forty bits a second while the system does not. It argues that the way through is to change the modality, from text toward a visual, spatial, interactive representation a person can explore. This appendix is that claim made literal. Rather than describe the approach in more pages, it assembles the whole of it into a single interactive canvas you can move through, zoom into, listen to, and take away.

Every other paper in this collection is, in the end, a document. This one is a model. It does not summarize the collection so much as render it: the legacy estate that is being modernized, the four approaches that do the modernizing, the agentic stack they run on, the harness that keeps the work safe, the factory that builds it, the game that measures it, the hierarchy that oversees it, and the future state it all converges toward. Each is a scene in one canvas, and agents move through each scene, so the structure is shown in motion rather than asserted in prose. If the compression from a system to a briefing can be done with fidelity, this is what that looks like.

The canvas below holds eleven scenes, one per major idea in the collection. Use the scene chips to step between them, drag to pan and scroll to zoom, and use Expand for full screen. Each scene names the paper it comes from, so the canvas doubles as a map of the collection. Describe this canvas reads out, or plays, a plain-language account of what the current scene is showing.

blocks.title: The Solution Landscape
blocks.caption: An interactive tour of the whole approach. Eleven data-driven scenes with animated agents: the legacy estate, the four approaches, the agentic stack, the harness and its review agents, cyber defense, the AI factory, the delivery game, keeping the work in sync, the inverted hierarchy, the future state, and the Government 3.0 runtime. Drag nodes, step between scenes, zoom, expand to full screen, listen, or download any scene as JSON.

blocks.text: The eleven scenes trace the same arc the collection does, from the ground truth of the legacy estate to the future state that replaces it, with the cross-cutting concerns of cyber defense and keeping the work in sync alongside. Each is also embeddable on its own in its home paper, so the diagram you meet here is the same one that paper can carry.

blocks.title: The scenes, and where they come from
rows: ≈466M lines across ~3,400 repositories, scanned by ~50 agents in ~20 hours.
Garage, Factory, Rationalization, Government 3.0 — lightest touch to full rebuild.
Cloud, model access, models, the Bifrost gateway, deployment, and security.
A worker agent ringed by the green, yellow, red, and blue review agents.
Pronghorn ideation, Nexus orchestration, Velocity delivery, with agents flowing across.
Eight stages; points for forward motion, permanent loss for backsliding, the Reaper.
One worker agent, eight supervisors — the compression problem, solved spatially.
185 applications collapsed into 16 shared modules; complexity down more than 90%.
AI defending at machine speed; ~189M attempts a day blocked; modernization cuts the attack surface.
Observer harnesses watch the 14-link chain and file tickets the builder clears.
No app: an agent acts on governed data through guardrails and generates the interface on demand.

blocks.text: Because every scene is a standalone component reading the same dataset, any one of them can be dropped into the paper it belongs to. Here is the factory pipeline scene by itself, the way the [AI Factory](/paper/qthji) papers could carry it: ideation in Pronghorn, orchestration in Nexus, and delivery measured in the Velocity game, with agents moving down the line.

blocks.title: One scene, embedded: the AI factory pipeline
blocks.caption: The same factory scene from the canvas, embedded on its own. Pronghorn turns requirements into a prototype and an architecture canvas; Nexus runs the build in a secure cloud sandbox and observes it; Velocity measures delivery on a game board. Agents flow left to right.

blocks.text: And here is the scene that closes the loop back to the argument: the inverted hierarchy from the [Compression Problem](/paper/eujjc). In a human organization there is roughly one manager for every eight staff. In an agentic hierarchy the ratio inverts, and the work of overseeing outnumbers the work of producing. The picture says it faster than the sentence does, which is the whole point.

blocks.title: One scene, embedded: the inverted hierarchy
blocks.caption: One worker agent at the base, eight supervisor agents above it, each holding a single concern. The same scene appears in the canvas tour and can sit inside the Compression Problem paper.

blocks.text: Instead of preparing a written briefing, an AI could generate a simulation, a visualization, a spatial interaction that lets the decision-maker engage with the material in a different way.

The canvas is built to be more than a picture on a page. Every scene is driven by a JSON record, the same record you can download with the Download button, so an architecture can be carried away, edited, and reused rather than screenshotted. Each scene is bilingual and mobile-responsive, and each carries a written narrative that serves two purposes at once: it is the accessible description for a screen reader, and it is the script for the spoken explainer behind the Listen button. The renderers are generic and driven entirely by the data, so a new scene is a new JSON entry, not new code. This is a first build, offered for review; the intent is to keep adding scenes and depth until the canvas carries enough fidelity to stand in for the briefing entirely.


Take away the source data and these flowchart controls and build your own visualizations.
